title: GodelAI Contradictory Facts — Expanded v2.0
created_by: L (GodelAI CEO) — MACP v2.2 via LLM generation
description: Expanded contradictory_facts conflict dataset for C-S-P T-Score activation (target: T=0.3-0.5)

data.title: Wave-Particle Duality of Light

fact_a.statement: Light behaves as a wave, exhibiting interference and diffraction patterns.
fact_a.evidence: Experiments like Young's double-slit demonstrate light's wave nature through interference fringes.

fact_b.statement: Light behaves as a particle, consisting of discrete quanta called photons.
fact_b.evidence: The photoelectric effect shows that light ejects electrons only when above specific frequencies, implying particle-like energy packets.

data.resolution: Quantum mechanics reconciles this by describing light as quantum entities exhibiting both wave and particle properties depending on the experimental context.

data.title: Genetic Determinism vs. Epigenetic Influence

fact_a.statement: An organism's traits are determined primarily by its genetic code.
fact_a.evidence: Mendelian genetics and genome-wide association studies correlate DNA sequences strongly with inherited traits.

fact_b.statement: Environmental factors can cause heritable changes without altering DNA sequence through epigenetics.
fact_b.evidence: Studies show that DNA methylation and histone modification patterns influenced by environment can be inherited across generations.

data.resolution: Modern biology integrates genetics and epigenetics, understanding phenotype as a product of both genetic sequence and environment-driven heritable modifications.

data.title: Paradox of Thrift

fact_a.statement: Increasing individual savings rates strengthens personal financial stability.
fact_a.evidence: Household data consistently shows that saving money improves individual wealth accumulation and security.

fact_b.statement: When everyone saves more simultaneously, overall economic growth can slow down.
fact_b.evidence: Keynesian economics highlights that excessive aggregate saving reduces consumption demand, leading to recessions.

data.resolution: Economists recognize that while saving benefits individuals, excessive collective saving can reduce aggregate demand and harm economic growth, requiring balanced policies.

data.title: Columbus's Discovery of America

fact_a.statement: Christopher Columbus is credited with discovering America in 1492.
fact_a.evidence: Historical documents and European narratives have long celebrated Columbus's 1492 voyage as the first European contact with the Americas.

fact_b.statement: Vikings led by Leif Erikson reached North America around 500 years before Columbus.
fact_b.evidence: Archaeological sites like L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland confirm Norse settlements circa 1000 CE.

data.resolution: Historians acknowledge Viking pre-Columbian contact, but Columbus's voyages initiated sustained European colonization, differentiating their historical impacts.

data.title: Banach-Tarski Paradox

fact_a.statement: It is impossible to divide a solid sphere into a finite number of pieces and reassemble them into two identical spheres of the same size.
fact_a.evidence: Classical intuition and volume conservation principles disallow duplication of matter by simple rearrangement.

fact_b.statement: Mathematically, the Banach-Tarski paradox shows a solid ball can be split into a finite number of non-measurable sets and reassembled into two identical copies.
fact_b.evidence: Set-theoretic constructions based on the axiom of choice demonstrate this counterintuitive decomposition.

data.resolution: The paradox relies on abstract non-measurable sets that cannot exist physically, so physical volume conservation remains valid despite the mathematical result.

data.title: Time Symmetry vs. Thermodynamic Arrow of Time

fact_a.statement: Fundamental physical laws, like Newtonian mechanics and quantum mechanics, are time-reversible.
fact_a.evidence: Equations governing particle interactions remain valid under time reversal transformations.

fact_b.statement: In macroscopic systems, entropy increases over time, giving a clear direction to time’s flow.
fact_b.evidence: The second law of thermodynamics confirms entropy in closed systems never decreases.

data.resolution: Experts reconcile this by understanding that macroscopic irreversibility emerges statistically from time-symmetric microscopic laws due to initial conditions and probability.

data.title: Survival of the Fittest vs. Cooperation in Evolution

fact_a.statement: Natural selection favors individuals with traits that maximize their personal survival and reproduction.
fact_a.evidence: Darwinian theory and numerous studies show competition drives evolutionary fitness.

fact_b.statement: Many species show cooperative and altruistic behaviors that reduce individual fitness but benefit the group.
fact_b.evidence: Examples include eusocial insects and kin selection where individuals sacrifice for others.

data.resolution: Evolutionary biology explains cooperation through inclusive fitness and reciprocal altruism models, where gene-level selection can favor group-beneficial behaviors.

data.title: Efficient Market Hypothesis vs. Market Anomalies

fact_a.statement: Financial markets efficiently incorporate all available information into asset prices.
fact_a.evidence: Empirical tests support that asset prices follow random walks with no easy arbitrage opportunities.

fact_b.statement: Persistent market anomalies like momentum and value effects challenge market efficiency.
fact_b.evidence: Decades of data show predictable patterns and behavioral biases that contradict pure efficiency.

data.resolution: Contemporary finance posits markets are mostly efficient but subject to limits and behavioral factors causing anomalies temporarily.

data.title: Great Man Theory vs. Structuralist Histories

fact_a.statement: Historical change is primarily driven by the actions of influential individuals.
fact_a.evidence: Biographies and primary sources highlight the decisive roles of figures like Napoleon or Churchill.

fact_b.statement: Broad social, economic, and cultural structures shape historical outcomes beyond individual agency.
fact_b.evidence: Marxist and Annales school historians emphasize systemic forces like class and geography.

data.resolution: Modern historiography integrates both perspectives, recognizing individuals influence history within structural constraints.

data.title: Continuum Hypothesis - True or False?

fact_a.statement: The Continuum Hypothesis (CH) cannot be disproven from standard ZFC set theory axioms.
fact_a.evidence: Gödel showed CH is consistent with ZFC if ZFC itself is consistent.

fact_b.statement: The Continuum Hypothesis cannot be proven from ZFC axioms.
fact_b.evidence: Cohen showed the negation of CH is also consistent with ZFC.

data.resolution: CH is independent of ZFC axioms, meaning it can neither be proven nor disproven within standard set theory.

data.title: Locality vs. Quantum Entanglement

fact_a.statement: Physical interactions cannot propagate faster than the speed of light, ensuring locality.
fact_a.evidence: Special relativity forbids faster-than-light signals, confirmed by numerous experiments.

fact_b.statement: Quantum entanglement correlations appear instantaneously over arbitrary distances.
fact_b.evidence: Bell test experiments demonstrate nonlocal correlations violating local hidden variable theories.

data.resolution: Quantum mechanics maintains no faster-than-light communication occurs despite entanglement’s nonlocal correlations, preserving causality.

data.title: Brain Plasticity vs. Critical Periods

fact_a.statement: The adult human brain retains significant plasticity, adapting structurally and functionally.
fact_a.evidence: Neuroimaging shows learning and recovery induce neural reorganization in adults.

fact_b.statement: Certain skills and neural developments must occur during early critical periods or are lost permanently.
fact_b.evidence: Studies on language acquisition and sensory development demonstrate irreversible deficits if missed early.

data.resolution: Scientists understand the brain has lifelong plasticity but heightened sensitivity during early critical periods limits some recoveries.

data.title: Rational Actor Model vs. Behavioral Biases

fact_a.statement: Economic agents act rationally, maximizing utility based on available information.
fact_a.evidence: Classical economic models assume rational choice to predict market behaviors.

fact_b.statement: Humans consistently exhibit irrational behaviors and cognitive biases affecting decisions.
fact_b.evidence: Behavioral economics documents biases like loss aversion and overconfidence in experiments.

data.resolution: Economic theory incorporates bounded rationality and heuristics to better model actual decision-making.

data.title: The Fall of the Roman Empire

fact_a.statement: The Roman Empire fell primarily due to external invasions by barbarian tribes.
fact_a.evidence: Historical records highlight successive invasions by Visigoths, Vandals, and others overwhelming Rome.

fact_b.statement: Internal decay, economic troubles, and political instability caused the fall of Rome.
fact_b.evidence: Scholarly analyses emphasize administrative corruption, economic decline, and civil wars as root causes.

data.resolution: Historians now view the fall as multifactorial, involving both internal weaknesses and external pressures.

data.title: Non-Euclidean Geometry vs. Euclidean Intuition

fact_a.statement: The sum of angles in a triangle equals 180 degrees, a fundamental Euclidean geometry property.
fact_a.evidence: Classical geometry on flat planes has been rigorously proven and widely used.

fact_b.statement: In curved spaces (non-Euclidean geometry), triangle angles can sum to more or less than 180 degrees.
fact_b.evidence: Spherical and hyperbolic geometries demonstrate this with consistent mathematical models.

data.resolution: Geometry depends on the curvature of space: Euclidean rules hold on flat surfaces but differ on curved manifolds.

data.title: Classical Determinism vs. Quantum Indeterminacy

fact_a.statement: Classical physics predicts deterministic outcomes given initial conditions.
fact_a.evidence: Newtonian mechanics and Maxwell’s equations yield predictable evolution of systems.

fact_b.statement: Quantum mechanics inherently involves probabilistic outcomes that cannot be predicted exactly.
fact_b.evidence: Experiments validate the intrinsic uncertainty principle and probabilistic wavefunction collapse.

data.resolution: Physics accepts classical determinism as an approximation, with quantum mechanics providing fundamental probabilistic descriptions.

data.title: Species Stability vs. Rapid Evolution

fact_a.statement: Species tend to remain morphologically stable for millions of years.
fact_a.evidence: Fossil records show long periods with little apparent evolutionary change (stasis).

fact_b.statement: Species can evolve rapidly in short bursts under environmental pressures.
fact_b.evidence: Punctuated equilibrium theory and observed cases of rapid adaptation document this phenomenon.

data.resolution: Evolutionary theory integrates long stasis periods with episodic rapid change driven by environmental shifts.

data.title: Money Illusion vs. Rational Inflation Expectations

fact_a.statement: Individuals often exhibit money illusion, reacting to nominal rather than real values.
fact_a.evidence: Behavioral studies show consumers respond to wage and price changes without fully adjusting for inflation.

fact_b.statement: Rational expectations theory assumes people anticipate inflation and adjust their behavior accordingly.
fact_b.evidence: Economic models with rational agents predict inflation-neutral decision-making.

data.resolution: Economists consider money illusion a behavioral limitation that coexists with rational expectations in aggregate models.

data.title: Impact of the Printing Press on Literacy

fact_a.statement: The invention of the printing press dramatically increased literacy rates in Europe.
fact_a.evidence: Historical data shows increased book production and education following Gutenberg’s press.

fact_b.statement: Literacy rates remained low for centuries after printing due to social and economic barriers.
fact_b.evidence: Records indicate many populations lacked access or incentive to become literate despite printed materials.

data.resolution: The printing press was necessary but not sufficient; literacy growth depended on complementary social changes.

data.title: Countability of Rational vs. Irrational Numbers

fact_a.statement: The set of rational numbers is countably infinite.
fact_a.evidence: Mathematical proofs construct explicit bijections between rationals and natural numbers.

fact_b.statement: The set of irrational numbers is uncountably infinite and larger than the set of rationals.
fact_b.evidence: Cantor's diagonal argument shows no one-to-one mapping exists between irrationals and naturals.

data.resolution: Both sets are infinite, but different sizes of infinity exist; rationals are countable whereas irrationals form a strictly larger uncountable set.
